Name: Oriental pied hornbill
Habitat: Lowland moist deciduous forests, forest edges, and plantations
Best_seen_at: Kaziranga National Park and Rajaji National Park
How_to_identify: Smallest Asian hornbill. Black upperparts and white underparts. Large ivory and black casque with a distinct black patch on the mandible tip.
Diet: Primarily fruit (figs), also insects and small reptiles

Name: Malabar pied hornbill
Habitat: Moist deciduous and evergreen forests, often near human settlements
Best_seen_at: Dandeli Wildlife Sanctuary and Tadoba-Andhari Tiger Reserve
How_to_identify: Larger than Oriental Pied. Features a massive ivory-yellow casque with a large, sweeping black patch. Black plumage with white belly and white tail-sides.
Diet: Mainly wild fruits, figs, and occasionally lizards

Name: Malabar grey hornbill
Habitat: Dense evergreen forests and coffee/rubber plantations
Best_seen_at: Thattekad Bird Sanctuary and Periyar National Park
How_to_identify: Endemic to the Western Ghats. It lacks a casque on the bill. Slaty-grey plumage with fine white streaks and a pale eyebrow.

Name: Indian grey hornbill
Habitat: Open woodlands, urban parks, and gardens
Best_seen_at: Sultanpur National Park and throughout the North Indian plains
How_to_identify: Brownish-grey body. Small, pointed casque with a dark tip. Long tail with a black subterminal band and white tips.
Diet: Figs, berries, insects, and occasionally flower petals

Name: Austen's brown hornbill
Habitat: Lowland evergreen and semi-evergreen forests with dense canopy
Best_seen_at: Namdapha National Park and Dehing Patkai Wildlife Sanctuary
How_to_identify: Mostly dark brown plumage. Males have white throats and cheeks, while females are entirely brownish-buff. Distinct white tips on the flight feathers.
Diet: Wild fruits, figs, insects, and small vertebrates

Name: Rufous-necked hornbill
Habitat: Moist montane forests at elevations of 600–2,000 meters
Best_seen_at: Namdapha National Park and Mahananda Wildlife Sanctuary
How_to_identify: Sexually dimorphic; males have brilliant rufous head and underparts. Females are all black. Large ivory bill with vertical dark ridges on the upper mandible.
Diet: Large drupes, wild figs, and occasionally squirrels/lizards

Name: Narcondam hornbill
Habitat: Tropical evergreen forests on a volcanic island
How_to_identify: Found only on one island. Males have a rufous head and neck. Females are black. Both have a prominent white tail and a corrugated casque base.
Diet: Primarily wild figs and other forest fruits

Name: Wreathed hornbill
Habitat: Subtropical and tropical moist lowland forests
Best_seen_at: Kaziranga National Park and Pakke Tiger Reserve
How_to_identify: Black body with a massive white tail. Males have a yellow gular pouch with a dark band; females have a blue pouch. The bill has a series of 'wreaths' or ridges at the base.
Diet: Mainly figs, also other fruit and animal prey

Name: Indian roller
Habitat: Open country, grasslands, scrub, and agricultural land
How_to_identify: Dull brownish-pink breast. In flight, wings reveal striking alternating bands of turquoise and deep purplish-blue. Blue crown and pale blue vent.
Diet: Beetles, grasshoppers, crickets, and small lizards

Name: Indochinese roller
Habitat: Moist open forests and plains with scattered trees
Best_seen_at: Kaziranga National Park and upper Assam plains
How_to_identify: Darker than the Indian Roller. Breast and face are deep purplish-lilac without white streaks. Wings are a darker, more uniform purplish-blue.
Diet: Large insects, grasshoppers, and small frogs
